steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content:
The sentence says the two centaur objects "exhibit striking differences in ______ object P/2019 LD2 (Atlas) is..." The blank must join the setup clause to a contrasting explanation ("while the..."). The first part makes a general claim; the second part illustrates it with specific contrasting details about the two objects. A colon can introduce that elaboration, and "while" signals the contrast within it.
  • Choice A — behavior. The: Creates two separate sentences. The second sentence then has no subject-verb setup that connects back to "striking differences." Breaks the elaboration the sentence requires. ✗
  • Choice B — behavior, while the: A comma before "while" creates a subordinate clause, not an elaboration of "striking differences." The contrast would be embedded rather than explained. ✗
  • Choice C — behavior; the: A semicolon connects two independent clauses but drops "while," losing the explicit contrast signal needed to show the two objects differ. ✗
  • Choice D — behavior: while the: The colon introduces the explanation of the "striking differences," and "while" marks the contrast between the two objects’ opposite behaviors (one active, one dormant). Both punctuation and conjunction are correct. ✓

steps.title: Apply the colon rule and confirm
steps.content: A colon after an independent clause introduces an elaboration or explanation. Here it introduces the contrasting details that explain the claimed differences. "While" then signals the two-part contrast. Only Choice D — behavior: while the supplies both. That matches Choice D — behavior: while the.
